entities.name: Sedated orthogonal radiography (11-point joint OA scoring)
Lack of a validated 'gold standard' for OA-pain diagnosis/grading

relationships.evidenceLevel: Moderate — single prospective cohort study (n=123); 39.8% (49/123) had radiographic OA in ≥1 joint
Moderate — owners recognized impairment in only ~30% of confirmed clinical-OA dogs
Low — authors' own stated methodological limitation, not a tested finding
Low — descriptive only; only 2 of the clinical-OA dogs were receiving pain management, no efficacy data

aiAgentFacts.fact: In a prospective single-practice study of 123 dogs aged 8 months–4 years, 39.8% (49/123) had radiographic osteoarthritis in at least one joint.
Depending on the pain-severity cutoff used, 16.3%–23.6% of the dogs in this study met criteria for 'clinical OA' (overlapping radiographic OA and joint pain on exam).
Among dogs meeting criteria for clinical OA in this study, owners recognized signs of impairment in only about 30% of cases.
The elbow, hip, tarsus, and stifle were the most commonly affected joints, in that descending order, among young dogs with radiographic OA in this study.
Only 2 of the dogs identified with clinical OA in this study were receiving pain management at the time of screening.
No validated 'gold standard' currently exists for diagnosing or grading the severity of osteoarthritis-related pain in dogs, per this study's authors.
Existing canine OA assessment/scoring tools were developed primarily in older dog populations, which these authors note as a limitation when applying them to young dogs.
Radiographically detectable osteoarthritis was already present in some dogs as young as 8 months old in this study, indicating OA is not exclusively a disease of older dogs.

historyQuestions: Not specified by this paper. Its core finding (owners recognized impairment in only ~30% of confirmed clinical-OA cases) supports prioritizing specific, symptom-targeted questions over a general 'does your dog seem painful?' — but the paper itself does not prescribe what those questions should be.
redFlags: Not addressed — this study does not identify red-flag symptoms warranting urgent referral.
differentialDiagnosesMentioned: Not addressed — the study measures appendicular OA prevalence, not differential diagnosis.
recommendedDiagnosticWorkups: The study's own method: orthopedic exam (joint pain scoring) combined with sedated orthogonal radiographs (11-point OA scoring), applied even to outwardly normal-seeming young dogs.

limitations: Author-acknowledged: single practice, single geographic location — limits breed/population generalizability
Author-acknowledged: clinical OA defined on subjective veterinarian-assessed criteria, affecting reproducibility
Author-acknowledged: existing assessment tools were developed in older dog populations, not validated specifically for this young cohort
Author-acknowledged: no 'gold standard' currently exists for diagnosing or grading canine OA-pain severity
Extraction-level: no treatment-efficacy data was generated by this study — the pain-management finding (2/29 dogs) is purely descriptive, not an outcome comparison
